content:
# Joshua Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 226-227 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_002_page_001.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), folio 164 — LXX

---

## Rahab and the Spies

**1** Joshua son of Nun secretly sent two men from Shittim as spies. "Go," he told them, "scout out the land — especially Jericho." So they went and came to the house of a woman named Rahab, a prostitute, and spent the night there.

**2** Word reached the king of Jericho: "Israelite men have slipped into the city tonight to spy out the land."

**3** The king sent a message to Rahab: "Hand over the men who came to you — the ones who entered your house. They are here to spy on the whole land."

---

**4** But Rahab had already taken the two men and hidden them. She said, "Yes, the men did come to me, but I had no idea where they were from. **5** When the city gate was being closed at dark, they left. I don't know which direction they went. Go after them quickly — you can still catch them."

**6** In fact, she had brought them up to the roof and concealed them under the stalks of flax she had spread out there.

**7** So the king's men pursued them along the road toward the Jordan, all the way to the river crossings. As soon as the pursuers had gone out, the city gate was shut behind them.

---

**8** Before the spies lay down for the night, Rahab went up to them on the roof.

**9** "I know that YHWH has given you this land," she told them. "Everyone here is terrified of you. The whole country has lost its nerve. **10** We have heard how YHWH dried up the waters of the Red Sea ahead of you when you came out of Egypt, and what you did to the two Amorite kings east of the Jordan — Sihon and Og — how you wiped them out completely. **11** When we heard it, our courage melted away. No one has any fight left because of you. YHWH your God — he is God in the heavens above and on the earth below.

**12** "So now, please — swear to me by YHWH. I have shown you kindness; now show kindness to my family. Give me a guarantee I can trust: **13** spare my father and mother, my brothers and sisters, and everyone who belongs to them. Save us from death."

**14** The men answered her, "We pledge our lives for yours. As long as you don't give us away, we will deal faithfully and kindly with you when YHWH hands the land over to us."

---

**15** Then she lowered them down by a rope through the window, since her house was built into the city wall — she lived right in the fortification itself.

**16** "Head for the hill country," she told them, "so the search party won't find you. Hide there for three days until they give up and come back. After that, go on your way."

**17** The men said to her, "We will be released from this oath you've made us swear **18** unless you do this: when we enter the land, tie this scarlet cord in the window you lowered us through. Bring your father, your mother, your brothers, and your whole family together inside your house. **19** Anyone who goes outside your doors into the street — their death is on their own head, and we bear no blame. But anyone inside the house with you — if a hand is laid on them, their blood is on our heads. **20** And if you tell anyone about our mission, we are released from the oath you've made us swear."

**21** "Agreed," she said. "Let it be as you say."

She sent them on their way, and after they had gone, she tied the scarlet cord in the window.

---

**22** The spies made their way to the hill country and stayed there three days until the search party returned. The pursuers searched the whole road but found nothing.

**23** Then the two men came back down from the hills, crossed the Jordan, and reported to Joshua son of Nun. They told him everything that had happened to them.

**24** "There is no doubt about it," they said to Joshua. "YHWH has given the entire land into our hands. The whole population is falling apart with fear because of us."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Joshua 2 — the outsider who saw what Israel could not

This is one of the great reversals in the Hebrew Bible. Joshua sends spies into Jericho to assess the enemy, and the intelligence they bring back is a confession of faith — not their own, but a Canaanite prostitute's. Rahab, who by every category in Israel's world should be the farthest from YHWH — foreign, female, a sex worker, a resident of the city under the ban — turns out to be the one person in the story who understands what is happening. She knows what YHWH has done. She knows what YHWH will do. And she acts on that knowledge with more decisiveness than the twelve spies managed in Numbers 13-14, where ten of them came back paralyzed with fear.

The chapter is structured as a spy thriller that turns into a covenant negotiation. The first half is deception and pursuit; the second half is theology and oath-making. Rahab drives both halves. She deceives the king, hides the spies, and then — in the quiet of the rooftop at night — delivers a speech that is, in miniature, Israel's own creed.

---

### Verse 1 — "a prostitute"

**זוֹנָה** (*zonah*) — The Hebrew is unambiguous. Rahab is a prostitute. The word *zonah* refers to a woman who sells sexual services; it is the standard term throughout the Hebrew Bible (cf. Genesis 34:31, 38:15; Leviticus 21:7; Judges 11:1, 16:1). The LXX Vaticanus renders **πόρνη** (*porne*), confirming the straightforward sense.

The Targum tradition softened this to *pundaqita* ("innkeeper"), and some interpreters have followed that reading, arguing that her house's location in the wall and its function as lodging suggest a hostel. But there is no lexical basis for this in Biblical Hebrew. The word *zonah* does not mean innkeeper; it means what it means. And the text does not flinch from it. Neither should the translation.

What matters for the narrative is not that Rahab is respectable — she is not — but that she is perceptive. The most marginal woman in Jericho has read the signs of the times more accurately than anyone else in the city, including the king. The Hebrew Bible has a consistent pattern of elevating unlikely people into the covenant story: Tamar, Ruth, Rahab herself. Matthew's genealogy of Jesus will name all three. Their outsider status is not incidental to their role; it is the point.

---

### Verse 1 — "spent the night there"

**וַיִּשְׁכְּבוּ שָׁמָּה** — Literally "and they lay down there." The verb *shakav* can refer simply to sleeping or lying down, and in context that is likely its primary meaning — the spies needed a place to stay. But the verb also carries sexual connotations throughout the Hebrew Bible (cf. Genesis 19:32-35, 30:15-16, 34:2; 2 Samuel 11:4), and the fact that they are in a prostitute's house makes the ambiguity audible. The text neither confirms nor denies anything beyond lodging. The LXX reads **κατέλυσαν** (*katelysan*) — "they lodged" — choosing the neutral sense.

I have translated "spent the night" to preserve the narrative's studied neutrality. The spies went to a prostitute's house and spent the night. Draw your own conclusions — the text declines to draw them for you.

---

### Verse 4 — "hidden them"

**וַתִּצְפְּנוֹ** (*vatitspenno*) — The Masoretic Text has a third-person masculine *singular* suffix: literally "she hid *him*," though two men are in view. This is the most notable textual crux in the chapter. The LXX reads **αὐτούς** (*autous*, "them"), resolving the difficulty with a plural.

The singular suffix may function distributively ("she hid each one"), or it may reflect an earlier textual tradition where a single spy was sent. PNEUMA translates with the natural English plural "them," following the logic of the narrative and the LXX witness, since a dynamic translation should not reproduce a grammatical anomaly that would only confuse the English reader without adding meaning.

---

### Verse 9 — Rahab's confession

**יָדַעְתִּי כִּי-נָתַן יְהוָה לָכֶם אֶת-הָאָרֶץ** — "I know that YHWH has given you the land."

This is extraordinary. Rahab uses the divine name — YHWH — not a generic "your god" or "a god." She speaks with the certainty of a prophet: *I know*. Not "I have heard rumors." Not "I suspect." *I know.* And what she knows is stated in the perfect tense: YHWH *has given* you the land. It is already done. The conquest has not yet begun, the walls are still standing, the king still sits on his throne, and Rahab speaks of the gift as accomplished fact.

The irony is devastating. In Numbers 13-14, Israelite spies entered the land and came back saying, "We can't do it — they are too strong." Now a Canaanite woman tells Israelite spies, "It's already over. You've already won. We know it." The people who should have had faith did not; the person who had every reason to resist has already surrendered — not to Israel, but to YHWH.

---

### Verse 10 — "the Red Sea"

**יַם-סוּף** (*Yam Suph*) — Literally "the Sea of Reeds." The traditional English rendering "Red Sea" follows the LXX's **ἐρυθρὰ θάλασσα** (*erythra thalassa*, "Red Sea"), which became the standard Western name. The Hebrew *suph* means "reed, rushwater plant" (cf. Exodus 2:3-5, where the infant Moses is placed among the *suph*). Whether "Reed Sea" or "Red Sea" is geographically correct has been debated for centuries. PNEUMA uses "Red Sea" because the dynamic equivalence goal is readability and recognition — every English reader knows the Red Sea crossing. The Hebrew term is noted here for transparency.

---

### Verse 10 — "wiped them out completely"

**הֶחֱרַמְתֶּם** (*hecharamtem*) — The Hiphil of *charam*, the verb of the *cherem* — the total devotion-ban. This is holy war language: everything is dedicated to YHWH by being destroyed. Sihon of Heshbon and Og of Bashan were defeated and placed under the ban (cf. Deuteronomy 2:34, 3:6). Rahab knows the technical term's meaning even if she does not know the theology behind it: everything was destroyed, every person, every city. She has heard the reports, and she is drawing the obvious conclusion about what awaits Jericho.

I have rendered this "wiped them out completely" because the dynamic equivalent must convey the totality and finality of the action. "Devoted to destruction" is theologically precise but does not land with the impact the Hebrew word carried in Rahab's mouth. She is not giving a theology lecture; she is explaining why everyone in her city is terrified.

---

### Verse 11 — "YHWH your God — he is God in the heavens above and on the earth below"

**כִּי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם הוּא אֱלֹהִים בַּשָּׁמַיִם מִמַּעַל וְעַל-הָאָרֶץ מִתָּחַת**

This is the climax of Rahab's speech, and it is a monotheistic confession on the lips of a polytheistic Canaanite. She does not say "YHWH is one god among many" or "YHWH is stronger than our gods." She says YHWH is God — in the heavens above and on the earth beneath. The formula echoes Deuteronomy 4:39, where Moses tells Israel the same thing. Rahab, without access to Moses or Torah, has arrived at the same conclusion by watching what YHWH does. Her epistemology is empirical: she has heard the reports, she has observed the pattern, and she draws the theological conclusion. This is natural theology at its most raw.

---

### Verse 12 — "kindness" (chesed)

**חֶסֶד** (*chesed*) — This word appears twice in the chapter (vv. 12, 14), and it is the hinge of the covenant negotiation. Rahab has shown *chesed* to the spies by hiding them; now she claims *chesed* in return for her family. The word means covenant loyalty, faithful love, the kindness that flows from obligation and relationship.

PNEUMA renders it "kindness" in verse 12 and "kindly" in verse 14, choosing the most natural English word that captures the relational warmth. "Steadfast love" (the formal tradition) is theologically richer but sounds alien in the mouth of a woman bargaining for her family's lives on a rooftop at night. Rahab is not delivering a psalm; she is cutting a deal. The English should sound like what it is: a desperate, shrewd, clear-eyed negotiation for survival, grounded in the only leverage she has — that she was kind to them, and now they owe her kindness back.

---

### Verse 12 — "a guarantee I can trust"

**אוֹת אֱמֶת** (*ot emet*) — Literally "a sign of truth/faithfulness." Rahab wants something concrete — not just a verbal promise, but a tangible marker that the oath is real. The *ot* is a sign, a token, a proof. The *emet* is truth, reliability, something you can stake your life on. The scarlet cord (v. 18) will become this sign.

I have rendered it "a guarantee I can trust" because that is what Rahab is asking for in plain language: something she can rely on when the swords come out.

---

### Verse 14 — "We pledge our lives for yours"

**נַפְשֵׁנוּ תַחְתֵּיכֶם לָמוּת** — Literally "our soul/life instead of you, to die." This is a compressed oath formula: the spies put their own lives as collateral. If they betray Rahab, their lives are forfeit. The infinitive *lamut* ("to die") provides the stakes.

PNEUMA renders this as a clean English pledge — "We pledge our lives for yours" — because the dynamic equivalent of a Hebrew oath formula is an English promise, not a word-for-word reproduction of the syntax.

---

### Verse 15 — "built into the city wall"

**כִּי בֵיתָהּ בְּקִיר הַחוֹמָה וּבַחוֹמָה הִיא יוֹשָׁבֶת** — "for her house was in the wall of the fortification, and in the fortification she was dwelling."

Rahab's house was physically embedded in the casemate wall structure of Jericho. Archaeological evidence from Bronze Age Jericho confirms that houses were built against and within the double-wall fortification system, with the space between the inner and outer walls serving as living quarters, often for people of lower social status. A prostitute living in the wall fits the social geography perfectly: she is literally on the margin, between inside and outside, part of the city's defense structure while being on its social edge.

This detail also explains the escape route: Rahab can lower the spies directly outside the city because her window opens onto the exterior face of the wall. There is no need to pass through the city streets or the guarded gate.

---

### Verse 18 — "this scarlet cord"

**תִּקְוַת חוּט הַשָּׁנִי** (*tiqvat chut ha-shani*) — The word **תִּקְוָה** (*tiqvah*) means "cord" or "rope," from the root *qavah*, "to twist together." But *tiqvah* is also the common Hebrew word for **hope** — the twisting together of strands into something that holds, something you can hang your weight on. The scarlet cord in the window is Rahab's hope made visible. The double meaning is almost certainly intentional: the cord is her hope, and the hope is a cord — thin, bright, and hanging from a window in a wall that is about to fall.

The color — **שָׁנִי** (*shani*), scarlet, the deep red of the kermes worm — has generated enormous interpretive tradition. Jewish readers noted the parallel with the Passover blood on the doorposts (Exodus 12): a marker on the house that spares the inhabitants from destruction. Christian readers extended this to the blood of Christ. The text itself makes neither connection explicitly. It simply says: scarlet cord, in the window. The reader's memory does the rest.

The LXX Vaticanus renders this as **τὸ σπαρτίον τὸ κόκκινον** ("the scarlet cord"), reading *tiqvah* purely as "cord." The wordplay with "hope" does not survive into Greek.

---

### Verse 21 — "she tied the scarlet cord in the window"

**וַתִּקְשֹׁר אֶת-תִּקְוַת הַשָּׁנִי בַּחַלּוֹן**

The chapter's final image is this: Rahab, alone, tying the scarlet cord. The spies have gone. The deal is struck. The invasion is coming. And she ties the cord — immediately, not waiting, not deliberating. The verb *tiqshor* is a simple narrative action: she tied it. The action is quiet, decisive, and loaded with everything the chapter has built.

She does not wait for the siege. She does not hedge her bets. She ties the cord now, in faith, while the walls are still standing. In this she resembles Abraham at his best: acting on a promise before the evidence arrives.

---

### Verse 23 — "everything that had happened to them"

**כָּל-הַמֹּצְאוֹת אֹתָם** — Literally "all the things that found them." The construction is unusual even in Hebrew: the Qal feminine plural participle of *matsa* ("to find") functions as a noun. Events *found* the spies — things happened *to* them rather than being things they actively discovered. The LXX renders **πάντα τὰ συμβεβηκότα αὐτοῖς** ("all the things that had happened to them"), confirming the passive-experiential reading.

PNEUMA translates naturally: "everything that had happened to them."

---

### Verse 24 — the spies' report

**כִּי-נָתַן יְהוָה בְּיָדֵנוּ אֶת-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ** — "For YHWH has given into our hand all the land."

The chapter ends where it began: with a report. But notice whose words the spies are repeating. In verse 9, Rahab said: "I know that YHWH has given you the land." In verse 24, the spies say: "YHWH has given the entire land into our hands." They are quoting her. The intelligence they gathered is a Canaanite woman's confession of faith. The spy mission produced no military data, no troop counts, no wall measurements. It produced Rahab — and Rahab was enough.

**וְגַם-נָמֹגוּ כָּל-יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ מִפָּנֵינוּ** — "And all the inhabitants of the land have melted before us." Again, Rahab's language (v. 9: "all the inhabitants of the land have melted"). The entire reconnaissance report is secondhand testimony from a woman the spies had no reason to trust and every reason to trust. She saw what they could not see because she was inside.

---

### The shape of the story

Joshua 2 is a spy narrative with a twist: the spy becomes the informant, and the informant becomes a convert. The chapter moves through three acts:

**Act 1 (vv. 1-7): The deception.** Rahab hides the spies, lies to the king, sends the pursuit team in the wrong direction. She is shrewd, quick, and ruthless. She risks her life for strangers she has decided to bet on.

**Act 2 (vv. 8-14): The confession and the covenant.** On the rooftop at night, Rahab delivers her theological assessment of the situation — YHWH is God, the land is given, the people are terrified — and cuts a deal. She trades intelligence for survival. The terms are precise: her family, inside the house, with the scarlet cord in the window.

**Act 3 (vv. 15-24): The escape and the report.** Rahab engineers the spies' escape through her wall-window, gives them practical survival instructions, and the spies return with a single piece of intelligence: YHWH has already won.

The chapter is preparation for the fall of Jericho in chapter 6, and when the walls come down, only one section will be left standing — the section with a scarlet cord hanging from a window. Rahab and her family will walk out alive (6:22-25), and she will live among Israel "to this day."

The Hebrew Bible remembers her. Matthew's Gospel puts her in the genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:5). The letter to the Hebrews names her alongside Abraham in the hall of faith (Hebrews 11:31). James holds her up as an example of faith that acts (James 2:25). A Canaanite prostitute from a condemned city became, by one act of risky loyalty, part of the permanent story.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 226-227, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Joshua 2 is written in the codex's three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks, beginning on page 226 (right column) and continuing through page 227. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, folio 164 (accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0168). The Vaticanus LXX is largely concordant with the Leningrad Codex throughout Joshua 2. Most notable divergence: the LXX reads a plural pronoun in v. 4 (αὐτούς, "them") where the MT has the singular suffix (וַתִּצְפְּנוֹ, "him"). No variant in the LXX requires revision of the PNEUMA translation.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
